coredata.dc:description: Asphaltene deposition is a significant issue in CO2-EOR, leading to reservoir damage and reduced efficiency. In this study, oil-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized to address asphaltene deposition issues, evaluating the inhibitory performance through laboratory experiments and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Experimental results demonstrated that CQDs effectively delayed the onset of asphaltene deposition from 45 vol% to 55 vol%, reduced the average particle size of asphaltene aggregates from 1200 nm to approximately 600 nm, and achieved an inhibition rate of 52.7 %. MD simulations revealed that CQDs reduced the mobility of asphaltene molecules and decreased the number of stacked asphaltene model molecules in aggregates from four to two, thereby lowering the risk of aggregation and subsequent deposition. Furthermore, the study showed that CQDs primarily inhibit asphaltene aggregation and deposition by weakening T-shaped or offset stacking interactions between asphaltene molecules. This effect was attributed to the strong interactions between CQDs and asphaltenes, with stabilization energies reaching up to −90 kcal/mol. Asphaltene molecules formed π-π interactions with CQDs through π-electron cloud overlap, dispersing the π-π interactions originally occurring between asphaltene molecules. This ultimately suppressed asphaltene aggregation. These characteristics suggested that CQDs offer a promising new approach to mitigate asphaltene deposition and have the potential to enhance oil recovery in low-permeability reservoirs.

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation and molecular simulations of CQDs for asphaltene deposition inhibition during CO2-EOR

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Asphaltene deposition is a significant issue in CO2-EOR, leading to reservoir damage and reduced efficiency. In this study, oil-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized to address asphaltene deposition issues, evaluating the inhibitory performance through laboratory experiments and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Experimental results demonstrated that CQDs effectively delayed the onset of asphaltene deposition from 45 vol% to 55 vol%, reduced the average particle size of asphaltene aggregates from 1200 nm to approximately 600 nm, and achieved an inhibition rate of 52.7 %. MD simulations revealed that CQDs reduced the mobility of asphaltene molecules and decreased the number of stacked asphaltene model molecules in aggregates from four to two, thereby lowering the risk of aggregation and subsequent deposition. Furthermore, the study showed that CQDs primarily inhibit asphaltene aggregation and deposition by weakening T-shaped or offset stacking interactions between asphaltene molecules. This effect was attributed to the strong interactions between CQDs and asphaltenes, with stabilization energies reaching up to −90 kcal/mol. Asphaltene molecules formed π-π interactions with CQDs through π-electron cloud overlap, dispersing the π-π interactions originally occurring between asphaltene molecules. This ultimately suppressed asphaltene aggregation. These characteristics suggested that CQDs offer a promising new approach to mitigate asphaltene deposition and have the potential to enhance oil recovery in low-permeability reservoirs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is a significant greenhouse gas contributing to climate change and causing substantial global impacts. As of 2023, global energy-related CO2 emissions have risen to 37.4 Gt [1]. Carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS) technology is considered crucial for achieving the Paris Agreement targets this decade and striving to limit global warming to 1.5 °C [2] due to its potential of enabling large-scale, sustainable, low-carbon use of fossil fuels and helping to build a low-carbon industrial system [3].

Low-permeability oil and gas resources are increasingly targeted for development due to their abundance [4]. To address the challenges of developing low-permeability reservoirs, such as high difficulty and low efficiency, gas flooding has emerged as a promising technique to enhance oil recovery from these formations [5]. Compared to other gas injectants like N2, air, and hydrocarbon, CO2 offers unique advantages in reducing crude oil viscosity and swelling oil volume [6,7]. In addition, the injected CO2 can be sequestered in the reservoirs, contributing to carbon emission reduction [8]. Consequently, this method, which is also known as CO2-enhanced oil recovery (CO2-EOR), stands out among various CCUS techniques. However, it poses a risk of asphaltene aggregation and deposition in reservoirs [9–11]. Asphaltenes, the most complex component of crude oil, are composed of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and small amounts of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and oxygen. They are insoluble in n-alkanes but soluble in aromatic solvents like benzene or toluene [12,13]. Upon contact with CO2, changes in reservoir temperature and pressure disrupt the dynamic stability of the dispersion system, causing asphaltene molecules to attract and aggregate, leading to deposition [14]. This results in wettability reversal, reduced permeability, and pore throat blockage, adversely affecting oil production [12,15,16]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop strategies to mitigate asphaltene deposition during CO2-EOR.

The use of chemical inhibitors is considered an effective measure to mitigate the damage caused by asphaltene deposition [17], including surfactants [18], organic acids [19], polymers [20], and ionic liquids [21]. They have specific molecular structures and functional groups that can interact with asphaltene molecules, inhibiting their aggregation and growth through steric hindrance [22–24]. However, there are several limitations such as high costs, environmental impact, and limited applicability across different crude oils. Recently, in the field of EOR, nanoparticles have garnered significant attention due to their unique physicochemical properties, such as surface effects, volume effects, and quantum effects, demonstrating great potential in inhibiting asphaltene deposition [25–28]. Ahmadi et al. [29] investigated the inhibitory effects of hydrophobic calcium oxide and commercial silica nanoparticles on asphaltene deposition, demonstrating that both materials exhibit robust adsorption capacities toward asphaltenes, thereby effectively mitigating their aggregation. Nevertheless, the practical application of standalone nanoparticles is constrained by agglomeration phenomena during deployment, which compromise their inhibition efficiency. Recent advancements have shifted focus toward nanocomposites, which mitigate this limitation through synergistic stabilization mechanisms, offering enhanced colloidal stability and sustained performance [30]. Bahaloo et al. [31] synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles with coatings, which can stabilize asphaltenes even under harsh conditions, attributed to the interaction between the coated oleic acid ester groups and asphaltene molecules, forming asphaltene-nanoparticle complexes. This inhibition of asphaltene aggregation and growth helped maintain the flocculates at a sufficiently small size, ensuring their stability within the liquid phase [32]. Nguyen et al. [33] demonstrated that polymer (AA-AMPS) functionalized magnetic nanoparticles could inhibit asphaltene deposition in microfluidic porous media, improving permeability by 75 %. Lu et al. [34] emphasized that aerogel nanoparticles also exhibited excellent asphaltene adsorption capabilities, effectively preventing deposition and leading to a higher asphaltene content in the produced oil while reducing the residual oil in the reservoir. Additionally, Tarboush et al. [35] found that in-situ synthesized NiO nanoparticles within heavy oil had a high adsorption affinity for asphaltenes. Similarly, Soleymani et al. [27] reported that in-situ synthesized crystalline nanoparticles could instantly adsorb asphaltenes, with adsorption capacity increasing with synthesis duration and the water content of the initial emulsion. Ahmadi et al. [36] synthesized a novel eucalyptus/CuO/Fe3O4/xanthan green nanocomposite for asphaltene adsorption, achieving maximum adsorption capacities (Qm) of 32.4675 mg/g at 48 h and 109.8901 mg/g at 96 h, effectively addressing asphaltene deposition issues in calcite and dolomite formations. Zinc oxide-based nanocomposites have also been identified as promising candidates for mitigating asphaltene deposition, owing to the small size of ZnO nanoparticles, which facilitates their transport through reservoir porous media. Additionally, ZnO nanoparticles significantly reduce interfacial tension and contact angle, thereby dispersing asphaltene molecules and preventing asphaltene deposition by approximately 12 wt% [37–41].

Although nanoparticles have shown significant advantages in addressing asphaltene issues, they still face challenges such as poor long-term stability (e.g., aggregation over time) and high costs, which hinder their viability for large-scale applications. Carbon nanomaterials, characterized by controllable morphology, abundant resources, and environmental friendliness, are increasingly being researched in the field of EOR [42]. However, there are few studies on their performance in inhibiting asphaltene deposition. Previous research has confirmed that reduced graphene oxide, multi- or single-walled carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanoparticles exhibit strong adsorption effects on asphaltenes. These materials could reduce the size of asphaltene aggregates due to strong interactions between asphaltenes and the surface sites of carbon nanostructures, including polar, π-π, and π-polar interactions [43–45]. Faraji et al. [46] investigated the impact of carbon nanotubes on inhibiting asphaltene molecular aggregation, revealing that the suppression primarily occurred through steric hindrance and adsorption mechanisms, which reduced the affinity of asphaltene molecules for association. Experimental and computational studies by Mohammadi et al. [47] demonstrated that the influence of carbon nanotubes on asphaltene precipitation in crude oil was governed by the strong π-π interactions between their respective electron clouds. Yu [48] explored the mechanisms of carbon nanotubes' effects on asphaltenes at the molecular level, highlighting that the strong interactions between carbon nanotubes and asphaltene molecules weaken the intermolecular interactions among asphaltene molecules, thereby influencing their self-aggregation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are typically defined as small carbon nanoparticles with particle sizes less than 10 nm and various surface passivation modifications [49]. Compared to other nanomaterials, CQDs have garnered significant attention due to their ultra-small size, environmental friendliness and low cost, offering broad application prospects in enhanced oil recovery (EOR). Kanj et al. [50] investigated the effects of CQDs on the static and dynamic wettability of carbonate reservoirs, revealing that the presence of CQDs could shift the wettability of reservoirs toward more water-wet conditions, favorable for EOR. Li et al. [51] developed a CQDs-based nanofluid for EOR, exhibiting excellent temperature resistance, salinity tolerance, and oil displacement performance. At a concentration of 0.1 wt%, the nanofluid reduced the oil–water interfacial tension to 13.4 mN/m. Sakthivel et al. [52] explored the impact of CQDs on foam stability, demonstrating that CQDs reduced the drainage rate of lamellae, delayed bubble rupture and coalescence, and prevented liquid drainage and film thinning, thereby enhancing foam stability. Moreover, some studies have utilized CQDs to inhibit or disperse asphaltene deposition and aggregation in unstable crude oils. Alemi et al. [53] found that at a CQDs concentration of 400 ppm, the onset of asphaltene precipitation was delayed from 26 vol% n-C7 to 37 vol% n-C7, attributed to the ultra-high specific surface area of CQDs. Zhang et al. [54] also reported that CQDs reduced heavy oil viscosity by altering the microcrystalline structural parameters of asphaltenes and dispersing asphaltene aggregates, thus lowering the stacking height of asphaltene aggregates. These findings suggest that, compared to nanoparticles like SiO2 and Fe3O4, CQDs have better structural compatibility with asphaltene molecules due to their size and surface properties, making them a promising choice for solving asphaltene precipitation problems [55].

This study presents a novel approach to mitigating asphaltene deposition. Oil-soluble CQDs were synthesized in one step via microwave method, and their properties were characterized using UV–Vis, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and contact angle measurements. Microscopic experiments and asphaltene dispersibility tests were designed to evaluate the inhibitory performance of CQDs nanofluids on asphaltene deposition. Additionally, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were performed to elucidate the molecular-level interactions between asphaltenes and CQDs.

In this study, polyether F-68 (analytical grade) and phosphoric acid (analytical grade, ≥85 %) for CQDs synthesis were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai. The toluene (analytical grade) and n-heptane (analytical grade) for asphaltene dispersibility tests were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai. The dispersant solution (an oil-soluble viscosity reducer called JRL) used to prepare CQDs nanofluids was internally produced in the laboratory. The crude oil used for asphaltene extraction was from Tahe Oilfield.

As shown in Fig. 1 , oil-soluble CQDs were synthesized in a one-step microwave-assisted process following [56]. First, 180 mg of polyether F-68 was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water, followed by 30 mL of phosphoric acid. The mixture was then subjected to microwave irradiation at 450 W for 9 min and subsequently cooled to room temperature. Next, 50 mL of deionized water was added, forming a suspended black-brown precipitate. To this, 50 mL of toluene was added to extract the CQDs, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2 h. The upper layer solution was then filtered to yield the CQDs-toluene solution. This solution was concentrated using a rotary evaporator at 90 °C and a vacuum of 0.8 MPa. Finally, the concentrated CQDs solution was evaporated to crystallization and dried to obtain black oil-soluble CQDs powder.

TEM (JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd.) was used to observe the aggregation state and particle size of CQDs and to capture TEM images. FTIR (TENSOR II, Bruker Corporation) was utilized for structural analysis to identify the surface functional groups. The wettability of CQDs was analyzed using a contact angle meter (HARKE-SPCAX3, Beijing Hake Test Instrument Factory), with the hydrophobicity evaluated based on the water contact angle.

According to the petrochemical industry standard NB/SH/T 0509–2010 [57], n-heptane was used for the extraction of asphaltene. Crude oil was mixed with n-heptane at a ratio of 1:40 (g/mL) and subjected to ultrasound in a water bath at 25 °C for 2 h. The mixture was then stirred for 24 h to reach dissolution equilibrium, followed by 12 h of settling in the dark, allowing black asphaltene to precipitate. After decanting the supernatant, the precipitated asphaltene was collected and washed with n-heptane at a ratio of 1:4 (g/mL) 2 to 3 times. The mixture was then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min, and left to stand overnight. This washing process was repeated 3 to 4 times until the asphaltenes exhibited a shiny black appearance. The collected asphaltenes were homogenized, finely ground using a mortar and pestle, and dried in a fume hood at room temperature to yield asphaltene solid powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of asphaltene deposition onset point
ce:section.ce:para: The onset of asphaltene precipitation was determined based on changes in absorbance. A 500 mg/L asphaltene-toluene solution was prepared and divided into six portions. Different ratios of n-heptane, CQDs, and toluene were added to each portion, with CQDs concentrations of 0 mg/L, 3.5 mg/L, 7 mg/L, 10.5 mg/L, 14 mg/L, and 17.5 mg/L, respectively. For each portion, n-heptane was added at concentrations ranging from 30 to 70 vol%, followed by the addition of toluene in corresponding proportions to form multiple mixed solution samples with an asphaltene concentration of 100 mg/L. After leaving for 24 h, the solutions were centrifuged, and the absorbance of the supernatants at 546 nm (see Fig. S1a) was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-4802, UNICO Instrument Co., Ltd.). The volume of n-heptane at which the absorbance exhibited a noticeable decrease was designated as the onset point marking the initiation of asphaltene deposition, and a determination curve was plotted [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of particle size of asphaltene aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: Four groups of solutions containing asphaltene, toluene, n-heptane, and CQDs were prepared, each with an n-heptane volume fraction of 60 % and CQDs concentrations of 0 mg/L, 3.5 mg/L, 7 mg/L, and 10.5 mg/L. The size of asphaltene aggregates was measured using a laser particle size analyzer (Delsa Nano C, BECKMAN COULTER), investigating the effect of CQDs concentration on asphaltene deposition. Additionally, the optical microscope (PX100, Phoenix Optical) was employed to directly observe the degree of asphaltene aggregation and the size distribution of the aggregates [53].

Five additional groups of similar solutions were prepared, each with a CQDs concentration of 7 mg/L and n-heptane volume fractions of 50 %, 55 %, 60 %, 65 %, and 70 %, respectively. The laser particle size analyzer (Delsa Nano C, BECKMAN COULTER) was again used to measure the size of asphaltene aggregates, this time exploring the effect of n-heptane volume on asphaltene deposition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the inhibition rate of asphaltene deposition
ce:section.ce:para:
0.02 g of CQDs was dissolved in 1 L of JRL and stirred for 1 h to ensure complete dissolution. The mixture was then ultrasonicated in a water bath at 25 °C for 2 h, followed by standing in the dark for 12 h to obtain CQDs nanofluid (CQDs-NF). Six groups of asphaltene-toluene-n-heptane-CQDs-NF solutions were prepared, with CQDs-NF addition levels of 0 %, 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, 4 %, and 5 % (wt%), respectively, while the n-heptane volume fraction was maintained at 60 %. The absorbance of the supernatants was measured after centrifugation of the samples. The asphaltene content was calculated using a standard curve (see Fig. S1b), and the rate of inhibition was calculated using equation (1) [59]
(1)

where R is the rate of inhibition; Wa0 and W are the deposition amounts without and with CQDs-NF, respectively, mg; C0 and C are the concentration of deposited asphaltene resulting from n-heptane without and with CQDs-NF, mg/L.

To elucidate the interaction between CQDs and asphaltenes at the molecular level, MD simulations were performed using LAMMPS software [60]. The initial model, as shown in Fig. 2 , was constructed using the AutoFF website [61]. Using the PACKMOL package [62], one CQDs model [63], ten asphaltene models [64], 1167 toluene models, and 1260n-heptane models were assembled into a simulation box with dimensions of 80 × 80 × 80 Å3. Under these conditions, the mass fractions of CQDs, asphaltenes, toluene, and n-heptane in the simulation box were 1.70 %, 3.35 %, 43.67 %, and 51.28 %, respectively. Notably, the volume fraction of n-heptane was 58.39 %. Periodic boundary conditions were then applied in all three directions. Van der Waals and long-range Coulombic interactions were calculated using Ewald summation with a cut-off distance of 10 Å. A stable initial configuration was achieved after energy minimization and geometric optimization. Subsequently, a 5 ns (ns) NVT ensemble simulation was conducted at 313.15 K. The simulated pressure was assumed to be nearly ambient, which was achieved by adjusting the molecular count of each constituent to attain the corresponding density. Finally, an additional 5 ns simulation under the same conditions was performed to ensure the accuracy and stability of the final results. Visualization was conducted using the Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) software [65]. Based on the analysis of the simulation trajectory files, the radial distribution function (RDF), mean square displacement (MSD), two-dimensional density distribution, interaction energy, and snapshots of asphaltene molecules were analyzed.

The interatomic forces were assigned using the Moltemplate package [66]. The bond potential for all molecules included bond stretching, angle bending, dihedral torsion, cross-terms, and out-of-plane interactions. The OPLS-AA force field [67] was employed for asphaltene, toluene, and n-heptane due to its suitability for organic compounds, while the CVFF force field [68] was used for CQDs to represent their structural properties accurately. The non-bonded potentials accounted for long-range electrostatic and short-range van der Waals interactions. Electrostatic interactions were represented using the Coulomb equation, while the Lennard-Jones potential described van der Waals interactions. Cross-interactions between different species were determined using the Lorentz-Berthelot mixing rules.

Fig. 3 a shows the TEM images and the particle size distribution of the synthesized CQDs. The TEM images revealed that the CQDs exhibited relatively uniform shapes, with particle sizes below 10 nm. Despite some size variation due to self-passivation, the CQDs maintained their distinct spherical/ellipsoidal shape without aggregation. The particle size distribution analysis showed that the sizes of the CQDs ranged from 0.5 to 4 nm, with an average size of 2.3 nm, indicated by a normal distribution fitting curve. As shown in Fig. 3b, the FTIR spectrum displayed characteristic absorption peaks: a broad peak at 3427 cm−1 corresponding to —OH stretching vibration, peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1 for symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of —CH2, respectively, a peak at 1713 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C=O, peaks at 1638 cm−1 and 1459 cm−1 associated with the skeletal vibrations of benzene rings, peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibrations of C=C and C–C, respectively, and peaks at 1200 cm−1 and 996 cm−1 for the stretching vibrations of C—O and C—O—C, respectively. These results confirm that the surface of the CQDs contains oxygen-containing functional groups including hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups. The structure includes both aryl and alkyl groups, and the core rich in isolated sp2 carbon clusters. Fig. 3c shows the contact angles of glass slides with water before and after coating with CQDs. The water contact angle of conventional hydrophilic glass slides was increased from 42.5° to 87.0° by the CQDs, changing the water droplets from a spread-out shape to a nearly spherical shape, indicating excellent hydrophobicity of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on the onset of asphaltene deposition
ce:section.ce:para: Supercritical CO2 is a non-polar solvent with solubility characteristics similar to those of n-heptane, making it well-suited for dissolving non-polar substances. In the context of CO2-EOR process, it can act as a precipitant for asphaltenes in the crude oil system, thereby inducing asphaltene deposition. To ensure that asphaltene precipitation was not influenced by other factors, this study used simulated oil system containing asphaltenes. Instead of directly using CO2 in experiments, n-heptane was utilized as the deposition inducer for asphaltenes. Fig. 4 illustrates the impact of CQDs on the onset of asphaltene deposition. With an increase in the volume fraction of n-heptane, the absorbance of the measured solution initially remained stable before decreasing. This phenomenon can be attributed to the disruption of solution equilibrium by the addition of n-heptane, leading to the gradual precipitation and aggregation of asphaltene particles into larger clusters. Eventually, these clusters deposited and separated from the system, resulting in a reduction in absorbance.

The addition of CQDs delayed the onset of asphaltene deposition, with the most significant delay observed from 45 V% (0 mg/L) to 54 V% (14 mg/L). This suggests a potential postponement of asphaltene deposition during CO2 flooding. The tendency of asphaltenes to aggregate was notably suppressed. In a specific concentration range, higher CQDs levels demonstrated an enhanced suppression effect. However, beyond a certain concentration threshold, this inhibitory effect diminished. At lower concentrations, CQDs likely interacted strongly with asphaltenes, forming CQDs-asphaltene aggregates that stabilized the asphaltenes in the solution and prevented their aggregation. As the concentration of CQDs exceeded a critical threshold, individual CQDs continued to interact with CQDs-asphaltene aggregates, leading to excessive growth and precipitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on particle size of asphaltene aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 a shows the variation in size of asphaltene aggregates under different CQDs concentrations when the volume fraction of n-heptane was 60 V%. Without CQDs, the size distribution of asphaltene aggregates ranged widely, from 400 nm to 3000 nm, with an average size of approximately 1200 nm. After the addition of CQDs, the size distribution range of asphaltene aggregates narrowed significantly, concentrating between 400 nm and 800 nm, reducing the average size to around 600 nm. Additionally, the size of asphaltene aggregates initially increased and then decreased as the CQDs concentration increased. This indicates that CQDs could effectively improve the size distribution of asphaltene aggregates, preventing the formation of large particles. However, beyond a certain concentration, the effectiveness of this control diminished, suggesting an optimal concentration for CQDs application.

As the volume of precipitant n-heptane increased, the amount of asphaltene precipitated from the system also rose proportionally. However, it remains unclear whether the deposition is primarily influenced by the size or the number of asphaltene aggregates, which necessitates further investigation. Fig. 5b shows the variation in aggregate size with different n-heptane proportions (50 V% to 70 V%) at a CQDs concentration of 7 mg/L, both with and without CQDs. Without CQDs, the aggregate size distribution was similar across the n-heptane range, between 400 and 3000 nm, with an average size of approximately 1200 nm. This suggests that the aggregate size distribution was independent of the n-heptane ratio, implying that asphaltene deposition was governed primarily by the number rather than the size of the aggregates. Moreover, with the addition of CQDs, the aggregate size distribution narrowed markedly, and the average size reduced to 600–700 nm, regardless of the n-heptane proportion.

To observe the aggregation degree and size distribution of asphaltene before and after the addition of CQDs, microscopic experiments were conducted on the solutions. The aggregation of asphaltene aggregates in different fields of view was examined, and the microscopic images are shown in Fig. 6 . Each column displays three fields of view. Without CQDs, asphaltene aggregates nearly fill the entire field of view, appearing as clusters with sizes around 1–2 μm. After the addition of CQDs, a significant decrease in the number of asphaltene aggregates was observed, with aggregate size reduced to below 1 μm, thereby greatly reducing the risk of asphaltene aggregation and subsequent deposition. Although some larger particles (1–2 μm) were still present in local areas, the observation of liquid samples under the cover slip indicated that asphaltene particles remained mobile. Changes in external conditions could cause smaller asphaltene particles to move and gradually form larger aggregates. Thus, the observed large particles may result from subsequent aggregation processes, suggesting that the original aggregate size in the solution was likely smaller. These microscopy results were consistent with the size distribution measured by dynamic light scattering, confirming that CQDs can effectively inhibit asphaltene aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs-NF on inhibition rate of asphaltene deposition
ce:section.ce:para: To address the challenge of injecting CQDs into reservoirs, this study prepared a CQDs nanofluid (CQDs-NF) and investigated its dispersive performance on asphaltene aggregates. The dispersion system used in the experiments was a self-made oil-soluble viscosity reducer (JRL) derived from vegetable oil, which is non-toxic, environmentally friendly, and easily degradable. Additionally, JRL has demonstrated effective viscosity reduction in heavy oil with high asphaltene content, and to some extent, can alleviate asphaltene deposition issues during crude oil development (see Supplementary Information for details). As shown in Fig. S2, JRL exhibited dilution and viscosity reduction effects and acted on the asphaltene components in heavy oil. Therefore, the addition of JRL enhances the dispersion of asphaltene in the system, with higher addition levels leading to improved dispersion effects and higher asphaltene deposition inhibition rates.

Considering the potential synergistic effect of CQDs and JRL in inhibiting asphaltene deposition, JRL was used as the dispersion system for CQDs-NF to further enhance the inhibition effect. Fig. 7 shows that the inhibition rate of asphaltene deposition by CQDs-NF increased initially and then decreased with the increase in dosage, reaching a maximum inhibition rate of approximately 52 % at a concentration of 2 %. At lower concentrations of CQDs-NF, the synergistic effect of CQDs and JRL dominated, significantly enhancing the asphaltene deposition inhibition rate. However, at higher concentrations, the interaction between CQDs and asphaltene aggregate particles caused flocculation, leading to a decrease in the inhibition rate. As the CQDs-NF concentration further increased, the dilution effect of JRL became dominant, improving the inhibition rate again. Therefore, considering the optimal performance and economic costs in practical applications, a dosage of 2 % for CQDs-NF is recommended.

Table 1 presents a comparison between our study's findings and those reported in previous work. The comparative analysis reveals that CQDs are highly effective in suppressing asphaltene deposition, underscoring their considerable potential for practical applications.

To elucidate the inhibition mechanism of CQDs on asphaltene deposition, MD simulations were conducted in two phases, a 5 ns equilibration to stabilize the system and a subsequent 5 ns production run for data collection. The final simulation snapshots, taken at the end of the production phase (after a total of 10 ns), are shown in Fig. 8 , clearly depicting the aggregation state of asphaltene molecules. In these snapshots, only the asphaltene and CQDs models are visible, with other models rendered transparent using the VMD package. Initially, asphaltene molecules were uniformly dispersed within the simulation box, but they eventually aggregated. In the absence of CQDs, severe asphaltene aggregation was observed, forming aggregates composed of four asphaltene molecules. With the presence of CQDs, while aggregation still occurred, only two asphaltene molecules were involved, indicating mitigation of asphaltene aggregation. The primary driving force behind asphaltene aggregation was the π-π stacking interactions between the aromatic cores of asphaltene molecules. These interactions arose from the electrostatic attraction between the negative π electron cloud and the positive σ framework of the aromatic rings. Common stacking modes include face-to-face or parallel, offset stacking or parallel-displaced, and edge-to-face or T-shaped configurations, which are repulsive, attractive, and attractive, respectively [75,76]. The two-dimensional density distribution of asphaltene molecules, calculated from 500 trajectory frames sampled at 10 ps intervals during the 5–10 ns production phase, further corroborates the aggregation behavior. As shown in Fig. 9 a and 9c (without CQDs), the normalized density map exhibits localized high-probability regions (red zones), indicating significant asphaltene aggregation. In contrast, with CQDs (Fig. 9b and 9d), the density distribution becomes markedly uniform (predominantly blue-green), reflecting effective dispersion of asphaltenes. The analysis was performed by discretizing the x-z plane into 0.25 Å × 0.25 Å grids using LAMMPS, with color intensity representing time-averaged molecular occupancy normalized to the maximum observed density.

The RDF represents the probability of finding other particles at a distance r around any specified central particle [77]. It can be used to analyse the effect of CQDs on asphaltene aggregation. As shown in Fig. 9e, without CQDs, the RDF of the asphaltene centroids exhibits two main peaks. The first peak appeared around 4 Å, indicating that the aromatic cores of asphaltene molecules interact through π-π stacking (van der Waals interactions), forming face-to-face aggregates [78]. This configuration represents the most stable dimer arrangement, as it minimizes the repulsion between negative π-electron clouds, promoting asphaltene aggregation. The second peak appeared around 8 Å, representing T-shaped or offset stacking, primarily due to hydrogen bonding between hydrogen atoms on highly electronegative atoms (typically oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur) and another highly electronegative atom [79]. Only the first peak was observed in the existence of CQDs, which suggests that CQDs primarily inhibit asphaltene aggregation and deposition by weakening T-shaped or offset stacking between asphaltene molecules. The RDF data were also used to calculate the aggregation number of asphaltene molecules at different distances. After adding CQDs, the average aggregation number decreased from 2.42 to 1.16, effectively reducing the risk of asphaltene aggregation. The MSD measures the movement and vibration of molecules in the simulation box, with a higher slope of the MSD curve indicating better diffusion [80]. Fig. 9f shows the MSD curves of asphaltene molecules during the simulation. Without CQDs, the movement and vibration of asphaltene molecules were higher than in the presence of CQDs. This is attributed to the strong attraction between asphaltene molecules and the surface functional groups of CQDs, which restricts the mobility of asphaltene molecules, enabling their stable dispersion in the system and preventing collisions and aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CQDs inhibiting asphaltene deposition
ce:section.ce:para: The infrared spectra of pure asphaltene and CQDs with adsorbed asphaltene were measured to identify the types of surface functional groups and compared with pure CQDs, as shown in Fig. 10 a. It is observed that CQDs and the asphaltene surface contain similar functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and benzene rings, indicating good compatibility between CQDs and asphaltenes. The adsorption of asphaltene on the surface of CQDs results from strong hydrogen bond interactions between the functional groups of CQDs and the active sites of asphaltene, as well as strong π-π interactions between the electron clouds on the aromatic rings of CQDs and asphaltene. Additionally, the interaction energy between CQDs and asphaltene molecules was measured using MD simulations, as shown in Fig. 10b. CQDs exhibited strong interactions with asphaltene, characterized by a maximum interaction energy of up to −90 kcal/mol when the system reached equilibrium. Compared to long-range coulomb interactions, van der Waals forces were more significant. These results suggest that the π-electron cloud overlap between CQDs and asphaltene molecules leads to π-π interactions, which disrupt the π-π interactions that would otherwise occur between asphaltene molecules, ultimately inhibiting asphaltene aggregation [81].

Therefore, CQDs can inhibit asphaltene deposition through two primary mechanisms. Firstly, asphaltene particles adsorb around CQDs due to their polar groups and surface charges, preventing aggregation and self-association, thus avoiding deposition. Secondly, through organic long chains, hydrogen bonds, and π-π interactions, CQDs bind with asphaltene, forming strong interactions and a stable layer on the asphaltene surface, thereby promoting the dispersion of asphaltene in the system. The microscopic mechanism of CQDs inhibiting asphaltene deposition is illustrated in Fig. 11 .

For CQDs-NF, the inhibition of asphaltene deposition is determined by the combined effects of CQDs and JRL. JRL, an ester compound with long carbon chains (detailed in the Supplementary Information), contains both polar ester groups and non-polar carbon chains, enabling it to interact with asphaltene through hydrogen bonding, solvation, and the principle of “like dissolves like”. Consequently, JRL can penetrate the layered structure of asphaltene and disassemble some of the overlapping aggregates, transforming larger asphaltene aggregates into smaller particles [82]. Subsequently, CQDs adsorb around asphaltene particles, preventing their deposition. Additionally, due to their nanoscale size, CQDs further interact with asphaltene aggregates that JRL cannot disassemble, promoting their dispersion. Therefore, at an optimal concentration, CQDs and JRL could synergistically enhance the inhibition of asphaltene deposition. However, beyond a certain concentration, the synergy weakens due to factors such as the excessive solvation layer thickness and flocculation induced by the CQDs.

This study explores the interaction between carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and asphaltenes, proposing a novel approach to mitigate asphaltene deposition during CO2-EOR. Oil-soluble CQDs were synthesized via a one-step microwave method, yielding particles with a size distribution of 0.5 to 4 nm and an average diameter of 2.3 nm. Their physicochemical properties were characterized, and dispersion experiments demonstrated that at an optimal concentration of 14 mg/L, CQDs effectively inhibited asphaltene aggregation, reducing the average aggregate size from 1200 nm to approximately 600 nm and narrowing the particle size distribution. Furthermore, at a CQDs nanofluid (CQDs-NF) concentration of 2 wt%, the inhibition rate of asphaltene deposition reached 52.7 %. Molecular dynamics simulations further revealed that CQDs reduced the stacking number of asphaltene molecules in aggregates from four to two. This inhibition was primarily attributed to van der Waals-driven competitive interactions, which weakened the T-shaped or offset π–π stacking between asphaltene molecules.

Notably, two limitations should be mentioned. Firstly, the potential aggregation of CQDs themselves was not considered in the experimental design. The following work will investigate the aggregation dynamics of CQDs at different concentrations and in various systems to elucidate their impact on asphaltene interactions. Secondly, molecular dynamics simulations were conducted under specific thermodynamic conditions that may not fully capture the complexity of real reservoir conditions. Further validation at the core or field scale is necessary to confirm the applicability of the findings.
